Within two years, Neema’s Tailoring Services will expand from a single sewing machine operation to a small tailoring shop with multiple machines, producing not only school uniforms but also shirts, skirts, and simple office wear.
Target Market
Parents of school children near two primary schools and one secondary school.
Small shops that sell uniforms but need a local tailor to produce them.
Students needing replacement uniforms during the school year.
Why this market is strong:
Children grow quickly → parents must buy new uniforms often.
Schools demand neat, specific uniforms.
Parents prefer reliable tailors who deliver on time.
Products & Pricing
Full school uniform set (shirt + skirt/trousers): TSh 15,000 – 18,000 depending on size.
Single items:
Shirt: TSh 6,000 – 7,000
Skirt/Trousers: TSh 8,000 – 10,000
Financial Snapshot
Cost per full uniform: TSh 11,000 (cloth, thread, transport).
Selling price: TSh 15,000.
Profit per uniform: TSh 4,000.
Short-term goal: 30 uniforms per month → TSh 120,000 profit.
One-year goal: 60 uniforms per month → TSh 240,000 profit.
Funding Needs
Startup capital required: TSh 250,000 for sewing machine, fabric stock, and accessories.
TSh 150,000 from personal savings.
TSh 100,000 from family loan.
Summary
This tailoring business is low-risk and high-demand, supported by the constant need for school uniforms. With reliable quality, fair pricing, and faster delivery than competitors, Neema’s Tailoring Services will quickly gain the trust of parents and schools, and grow into a profitable, expanding enterprise.

Executive Summary should always “sell the idea” in a clear, simple, and confident way.
Keep sentences short — avoid long explanations.
Always include mission, vision, customers, pricing, funding, and growth snapshot.
Business Details
1. Business Idea
Neema’s Tailoring Services will focus on producing school uniforms that are affordable, neat, and ready on time. Parents often struggle to find reliable tailors who deliver quickly before school reopening dates. By offering fair prices, neat finishing, and fast turnaround (within 3 days), Neema will stand out from other tailors.
👉 Unique Point: Every uniform will be checked for correct measurements and ironed before delivery, giving parents confidence in the quality.
2. Customers (Target Market)
The main customers are:
Parents of school children (primary and secondary).
Local shops that sell uniforms but need tailors to supply them.
Students themselves (especially older ones) who need replacements mid-term.
Customer Needs:
Affordable prices.
Correct school colors and designs.
On-time delivery (before opening dates).
Long-lasting quality (uniforms must survive daily washing and use).
👉 This customer base is strong because school uniforms are not optional — they are a constant need.
3. Competition Analysis
There are three main tailors near the two schools:
Tailor A – Sells uniforms at TSh 16,000.
Weakness: often delivers late.
Tailor B – Charges TSh 14,000 but uses cheap fabric.
Weakness: uniforms wear out quickly.
Tailor C – Reliable but too busy, customers wait weeks.
My Competitive Advantage:
Fair price (TSh 15,000) with better quality cloth.
Delivery within 3 days.
Neat finishing and ironing included.
More personal attention to parents’ needs.
Summary of Business Details
Neema’s Tailoring Services will succeed because it focuses on a daily, high-demand need (school uniforms), targets parents and local shops, and stands out by providing faster delivery, fair pricing, and neat finishing compared to competitors.

Always explain the business idea in one short paragraph.
Describe customers in clear, everyday language (avoid business jargon).
Show competitors’ weaknesses honestly but respectfully.
Highlight at least one unique advantage to stand out.
Operations and Marketing
1. Operations Plan
Daily Workflow:
8:00am – 12:00pm: Measuring customers and cutting fabric.
12:00pm – 5:00pm: Sewing uniforms, attaching buttons/zippers, ironing finished pieces.
5:00pm – 6:00pm: Delivering uniforms or preparing for the next day.
Work Location:
Home workshop near the primary schools (low-cost setup).
Suppliers:
Main supplier: Kariakoo fabric shops (bulk uniform cloth).
Backup supplier: Local market fabric stalls.
Staffing:
Owner (Neema) – sewing and customer management.
Helper (part-time) – ironing, cutting threads, cleaning.
Tools & Equipment:
Sewing machine (1 unit, electric/manual).
Iron, scissors, measuring tape, fabric chalk, rulers.
Future purchase: Second sewing machine for faster work.
2. Marketing Plan
Marketing Channels:
Word of Mouth
Satisfied parents will recommend Neema to other families.
Schoolteachers may also refer parents to her service.
Posters & Flyers
Simple posters placed near schools, stationery shops, and markets.
Flyers distributed to parents during school opening season.
Social Media (WhatsApp & Facebook)
Before-and-after pictures of uniforms.
Announcements of special offers (e.g., “School reopening discount – order now!”).
Jinnar Platform
Business profile with photos and reviews.
Visibility for parents searching online for tailors.
Customer Attraction Strategies:
Free ironing with every uniform (added value).
Delivery option for bulk orders.
“Repeat buyer bonus” → small discount for parents ordering 3+ uniforms.
Summary of Operations and Marketing
Neema’s Tailoring Services will run on a structured daily routine that ensures timely delivery and neat finishing. Marketing will focus on low-cost, high-impact methods like word of mouth, flyers, social media, and the Jinnar platform. By combining reliable operations with effective marketing, the business will grow quickly in the school uniform market.

Always explain the daily routine clearly so it looks realistic.
List tools and suppliers to show readiness.
Marketing should focus on simple, affordable methods that any worker can copy.
Financial Plan
1. Startup Costs
Initial money required to set up the tailoring business:
2. Cost per Uniform (One Full Set)
3. Pricing & Profit
Selling price per uniform: TSh 15,000
Cost per uniform: TSh 11,000
Profit per uniform: TSh 4,000
4. Profit Projections
5. Funding Plan

Personal savings: TSh 150,000

Summary of Financial Plan
Neema’s Tailoring Services is a low-cost, high-demand business. With only TSh 250,000 startup capital, the business can generate up to TSh 240,000 monthly profit within one year, and steadily expand by reinvesting profits.

Always use cost-per-unit + profit tables so numbers are clear.
Show realistic, not exaggerated, profit projections.
Mention funding source to prove startup readiness.
Risk, Team, and Growth
1. Team and Partnerships
Neema’s Tailoring Services will run with a small but efficient team:
Owner (Neema): Tailoring, customer service, and finances.
Helper (part-time): Ironing, trimming threads, helping with measurements.
Suppliers:
Main fabric supplier – Kariakoo market (bulk orders).
Backup supplier – Local shops near school.
Benefit of Partnerships: Steady material supply, shared workload, and faster production during busy school-opening seasons.
2. Risks and Solutions
👉 Key Point: A good tailor is not only prepared with a needle and thread, but also with solutions for unexpected problems.
3. Growth Plan
Short-Term (0–6 months):
Build trust with parents near the two schools.
Reach 30 uniforms per month.
Buy small stock of cloth to speed up delivery.
Medium-Term (6–12 months):
Reach 45–60 uniforms per month.
Buy a second sewing machine to handle more orders.
Begin supplying uniforms to one stationery shop.
Long-Term (1–2 years):
Open a small tailoring shop near the schools.
Train 1–2 apprentices for larger capacity.
Expand services to office wear and simple dresses.

Neema’s Tailoring Services is a reliable, demand-driven business that solves a clear problem for parents: affordable, neat, and on-time school uniforms. With careful operations, realistic financial goals, and a strong growth plan, this small workshop has the potential to become a trusted tailoring shop in the community.

Always show team setup — even if it’s just 1–2 people.
Use a risk table to make challenges & solutions easy to see.
Growth should be step-by-step (short, medium, long-term).
End with a positive but realistic conclusion.
Sample Business Plan # 3
Cover Page
BUSINESS NAME
👉 Juma’s Fast Delivery & Transport Services
Tagline (Optional):
👉 “Safe, Fast, and Reliable Rides & Deliveries”
Prepared By:
👉 Juma Mwakyusa
Date:
👉 March 2025
Contact Information:
👉 Phone: 07XX-XXXXXX
👉 Email: [if available]

Keep the business name big and centered on the page.
Add a tagline — makes the business feel professional.
Optionally insert a small motorbike icon/image to reinforce the brand identity.
Minimal text — Cover Page is meant to look clean.
Executive Summary
Business Overview
Juma’s Fast Delivery & Transport Services is a boda boda (motorbike) business based in Morogoro. The service provides safe, fast, and reliable rides for passengers and small parcel deliveries for shops and individuals. With the growth of small businesses and busy urban life, demand for quick, affordable transport is increasing every day.
Mission Statement
“To provide safe and reliable transport and delivery services that save time and make life easier for the community.”
Vision Statement
In two years, Juma’s Fast Delivery & Transport Services will expand from one motorbike to a fleet of 3–4 boda bodas, employing other riders, and becoming a trusted delivery partner for local shops and offices.
Target Market
Passengers (workers, students, and traders) needing quick transport around town.
Small shops & retailers who need parcels delivered to customers.
Local offices requiring urgent document delivery.
Why this market is strong:
Transport demand is daily and constant.
Small businesses prefer boda riders for fast, low-cost delivery.
Offices trust riders who are reliable and professional.
Products & Pricing
Passenger rides: Average TSh 2,000–5,000 per trip (depending on distance).
Parcel delivery: Average TSh 3,000–8,000 per delivery.
Daily average earnings goal: TSh 30,000–40,000 gross.
Financial Snapshot
Daily operating cost (fuel, maintenance, airtime): ~TSh 10,000.
Net profit target: ~TSh 20,000–30,000 daily.
Monthly net profit goal: TSh 520,000 – 780,000 (based on 26 working days).
Funding Needs
Startup capital required: TSh 1,200,000 for motorbike purchase, helmet, reflector jacket, and licenses.
TSh 600,000 from personal savings.
TSh 600,000 from VICOBA loan.
Summary
With daily demand, flexible services (passengers + parcels), and professional customer care, Juma’s Fast Delivery & Transport Services is a low-cost, high-return business. The plan starts small with one boda boda but has strong potential to expand into a multi-bike delivery and transport service within two years.

Executive Summary must show: business overview, mission, vision, customers, pricing, funding, and growth.
Keep it clear and number-based so anyone reading can trust the plan.
Avoid long storytelling — make it practical and to the point.
Business Details
1. Business Idea
Juma’s Fast Delivery & Transport Services provides affordable, safe, and reliable boda rides and delivery services in Morogoro. The business will serve both individual passengers who need fast transport and small businesses that need goods delivered quickly.
👉 Unique Point: Juma will combine professional customer care, fair pricing, and reliability to stand out from casual riders who often delay or overcharge.
2. Customers (Target Market)
Main customer groups include:
Passengers: Office workers, students, traders, and travelers needing quick trips within town.
Small shops & retailers: Need a trusted rider to deliver goods (clothes, groceries, electronics).
Local offices & schools: Need urgent documents, parcels, or small packages delivered.
Why these customers will buy:
They need fast service daily.
They want safety and reliability.
They are tired of riders who are unreliable or inconsistent with pricing.
3. Competition Analysis
In Morogoro, there are many boda riders, but most lack professionalism.
Competitors:
Informal boda riders at bus stands → cheap, but unsafe and unreliable.
A few organized boda groups → better service, but often too busy.
My Competitive Advantage:
Consistent, fair pricing (no overcharging).
Professional image (helmet, reflector, ID card).
Customer-first approach (polite, on time, trustworthy).
Flexibility (handles both rides and deliveries).
Summary of Business Details
This boda business is based on a daily, unavoidable need: transport and delivery. Juma’s service will win customers by being faster, safer, and more professional than most competitors.

Always describe the idea in simple words.
Break customers into clear groups (e.g., passengers, shops, offices).
Compare fairly with competitors and show your difference.
Operations and Marketing
1. Operations Plan
Daily Workflow:
6:00am – 9:00am: Passenger trips for workers and students.
9:00am – 4:00pm: Parcel and shop deliveries (groceries, clothes, documents).
4:00pm – 9:00pm: Evening passenger trips (traders, shoppers, travelers).
End of day: Refuel, clean bike, prepare for next day.
Work Location:
Operates mainly around Morogoro bus stand, schools, shops, and offices.
Tools & Equipment:
Motorbike (well-maintained).
Helmet, reflector jacket, and gloves for safety.
Smartphone for customer calls and WhatsApp orders.
Suppliers & Support Services:
Fuel station (daily).
Mechanic (weekly check-ups).
Airtel/Tigo for airtime and mobile money.
Staffing:
Owner (Juma) – main rider.
In future, employ 1–2 additional riders when expanding.
2. Marketing Plan
Marketing Channels:
Word of Mouth
Passengers recommend Juma to friends if service is safe and reliable.
Business Cards & Flyers
Simple cards with phone number for quick contact.
Social Media (WhatsApp & Facebook):
Post “Quick delivery available” ads in local groups.
Share satisfied customer reviews.
Jinnar Platform
Profile with photo, ratings, and description.
Visibility to customers searching for safe delivery riders.
Customer Attraction Strategies:
Fair, consistent pricing → no haggling, customers trust him.
Professional image → helmet, reflector jacket, polite greeting.
Reliable timing → “On time or earlier” as a service promise.
Loyalty bonus → 10th delivery free or discount for repeat shops.
Summary of Operations and Marketing
Juma’s Fast Delivery & Transport Services will run on a clear daily routine, keeping the motorbike safe and reliable. Marketing will rely on low-cost, practical methods — word of mouth, business cards, WhatsApp, and the Jinnar platform. By being professional and consistent, Juma will attract daily customers and long-term partnerships with shops and offices.

Operations = daily schedule, tools, suppliers.
Marketing = simple, low-cost, repeatable methods.
Highlight at least one “promise” that makes the business unique.
Financial Plan
1. Startup Costs
Initial money needed to start the boda service:
2. Daily Operating Costs
3. Income and Profit
Average Pricing:
Passenger rides: TSh 2,000–5,000 per trip.
Parcel delivery: TSh 3,000–8,000 per trip.
Example Daily Sales:
8 passenger trips (avg. 3,000 each) = TSh 24,000
4 deliveries (avg. 4,000 each) = TSh 16,000
Total Daily Income = 40,000
Profit Calculation:
Income: 40,000
Costs: 10,000
Net Daily Profit = 30,000
4. Profit Projections
5. Funding Plan
Total startup requirement = TSh 1,200,000
Personal savings: TSh 600,000
VICOBA loan: TSh 600,000
6. Financial Goals
Short-term (3 months): Average profit TSh 20,000/day → TSh 520,000 monthly.
Medium-term (6–12 months): Build steady base of shop deliveries → profit 650,000 monthly.
Long-term (2 years): Buy second motorbike and employ another rider.
Summary of Financial Plan
Juma’s Fast Delivery & Transport Services is a low-cost, high-demand business. With TSh 1.2 million startup, the service can generate up to TSh 9 million yearly profit. By saving and reinvesting, the business can expand into a small fleet within 2 years.

Always show startup vs daily costs separately.
Profit projections should be step-by-step (small → medium → expansion).
Funding source must be clear and realistic.
Risk, Team, and Growth
1. Team and Partnerships
At the beginning, Juma will operate mostly alone but rely on partnerships to strengthen the business:
Owner (Juma): Rides, deliveries, finances, customer service.
Mechanic (partner): Regular bike servicing to reduce breakdowns.
Boda association/VICOBA group: Provides loans and community support.
Shops & offices (clients): Become repeat partners for deliveries.
Future Team Plan:
Add one more rider within 2 years → expand capacity.
Employ a part-time assistant for customer calls and order coordination.
2. Risks and Solutions
👉 Being professional, safe, and reliable is Juma’s strongest defense against risks.
3. Growth Plan
Short-Term (0–6 months):
Build customer base of 20+ repeat clients (shops, offices).
Reach average daily profit of TSh 25,000.
Medium-Term (6–12 months):
Strengthen parcel delivery side of business.
Add savings for a second motorbike.
Begin branding (reflector jacket with business name).
Long-Term (1–2 years):
Buy a second and third motorbike → create a fleet.
Employ 2–3 riders to cover larger area.
Become a trusted delivery partner for small businesses in Morogoro.

Juma’s Fast Delivery & Transport Services is a daily-need business with strong growth potential. By focusing on safety, reliability, and professionalism, Juma can win loyal customers and grow from one motorbike into a small fleet within 2 years.

Risk tables make problems & solutions easy to understand.
Growth plans should always be in short, medium, and long steps.
End with a positive but realistic conclusion.
Qs&As Section
📘 Lesson 1: What is a Business Plan?
15 Questions & Answers
Part A: Multiple Choice (10 Questions)
Q1. What is a business plan compared to in this lesson?
A) A recipe
B) A map
C) A house
D) A bicycle
Answer: B) A map
Q2. Why is a business plan like a map?
A) It helps you avoid paying taxes.
B) It shows where you want to go and how to get there.
C) It makes your business look bigger than it is.
D) It keeps competitors away.
Answer: B) It shows where you want to go and how to get there.
Q3. Which of the following is a main benefit of having a business plan?
A) It guarantees a bank loan.
B) It prevents competition.
C) It reduces confusion and wasted money.
D) It eliminates all risks.
Answer: C) It reduces confusion and wasted money.
Q4. True or False: A business plan must always be more than 20 pages long.
Answer: False — even a one-page plan can be useful.
Q5. Who needs a business plan?
A) Only large companies
B) Only shop owners
C) Every worker or business owner
D) Only people with loans
Answer: C) Every worker or business owner
Q6. Which of these is NOT a purpose of a business plan?
A) To attract customers
B) To confuse competitors
C) To guide daily decisions
D) To plan for growth
Answer: B) To confuse competitors
Q7. A business plan helps a worker to:
A) Avoid all challenges.
B) Remember important details.
C) Copy other businesses.
D) Stop marketing.
Answer: B) Remember important details.
Q8. What happens if you only keep your plan “in your head” and not written down?
A) You will always remember everything.
B) Customers will trust you more.
C) You may forget important details.
D) It makes the plan stronger.
Answer: C) You may forget important details.
Q9. What is the minimum form a business plan can take to be helpful?
A) 1 page
B) 10 pages
C) 50 pages
D) 100 pages
Answer: A) 1 page
Q10. Which statement is TRUE?
A) Business plans are only for people with money.
B) Business plans are only for government projects.
C) Business plans are useful for all types of workers.
D) Business plans are optional and unnecessary.
Answer: C) Business plans are useful for all types of workers.
Part B: Short Answer (5 Questions)
Q11. In your own words, explain what a business plan is.
Answer: A business plan is a written guide that shows what your business will do, how it will work, and where you want it to go.
Q12. Name two benefits of having a written business plan.
Answer: It reduces confusion and wasted money; it helps guide daily decisions.
Q13. Why can even a one-page business plan make a big difference?
Answer: Because it forces you to think clearly about your goals and helps you explain your business to others.
Q14. Who can benefit from writing a business plan — give an example.
Answer: Any worker can benefit — for example, a food vendor writing down daily costs and profit goals.
Q15. Fill in the blank: A business plan is like a __________ because it shows where you want to go and how to get there.
Answer: Map
📘 Lesson 2: Why Every Worker Needs a Plan
15 Questions & Answers
Part A: Multiple Choice (10 Questions)
Q1. Why does every worker need a plan?
A) To show off to others
B) To guide decisions and avoid mistakes
C) To make the plan longer
D) To confuse competitors
Answer: B) To guide decisions and avoid mistakes
Q2. Which of the following is a risk of working without a plan?
A) More savings
B) Faster growth
C) Wasted money and confusion
D) More customer loyalty
Answer: C) Wasted money and confusion
Q3. True or False: Only shop owners need a business plan, not workers like tailors or boda riders.
Answer: False — every worker benefits from a plan.
Q4. Which of these is NOT a benefit of having a plan?
A) It helps you use money wisely.
B) It makes you appear professional.
C) It guarantees you will never face risks.
D) It helps you explain your business to others.
Answer: C) It guarantees you will never face risks.
Q5. A plan helps workers to:
A) Forget less and act with clarity
B) Spend without control
C) Avoid all competition
D) Ignore customer needs
Answer: A) Forget less and act with clarity
Q6. Which of these is an example of a worker using a plan?
A) A tailor guessing prices daily
B) A boda rider deciding routes randomly
C) A food vendor writing down daily costs and profit goals
D) A mason starting work without knowing material needs
Answer: C) A food vendor writing down daily costs and profit goals
Q7. What do customers think when they see a worker has a plan?
A) The worker is professional and reliable
B) The worker is confused
C) The worker will never change prices
D) The worker doesn’t need marketing
Answer: A) The worker is professional and reliable
Q8. A plan helps workers use their profits for:
A) Buying random items
B) Reinvestment and growth
C) Hiding from competition
D) Avoiding savings
Answer: B) Reinvestment and growth
Q9. Which worker is most likely to grow?
A) One who keeps working without goals
B) One who has a clear plan for profits and growth
C) One who depends on luck
D) One who copies competitors blindly
Answer: B) One who has a clear plan for profits and growth
Q10. True or False: A business plan helps workers not only survive, but also grow over time.
Answer: True
Part B: Short Answer (5 Questions)
Q11. In your own words, explain why a plan is important for every worker.
Answer: A plan helps guide daily decisions, reduce mistakes, and show the path for growth.
Q12. Give two examples of workers who need a plan.
Answer: A tailor planning school uniform orders; a boda rider planning daily fuel costs and profit goals.
Q13. What happens when a worker spends money without a plan?
Answer: They may waste money, run out of capital, or fail to grow.
Q14. How does having a plan make a worker look more professional?
Answer: Customers see that the worker is organized, serious, and trustworthy.
Q15. Fill in the blank: A worker with a plan is more likely to ________ and not just survive.
Answer: Grow
📘 Lesson 3: The Main Parts of a Business Plan
Section 1: Business Idea (10 Q&A)
Q1. What is a business idea?
Answer: It is the product or service you plan to offer to customers.
Q2. Why is a clear business idea important?
Answer: It guides everything else — costs, pricing, customers, and marketing.
Q3. Which of these is a good example of a clear business idea?
A) “I will sell something.”
B) “I will sell affordable school uniforms to parents.”
C) “I will try many things.”
Answer: B) “I will sell affordable school uniforms to parents.”
Q4. True or False: A business idea can be successful without customers.
Answer: False — no customers = no business.
Q5. Which worker has a stronger business idea?
A) Food seller: “I cook food sometimes.”
B) Food seller: “I will sell rice and beans at the bus stand for workers at lunchtime.”
Answer: B
Q6. What makes a business idea realistic?
Answer: When it matches customer needs, is affordable to start, and fits the worker’s skills.
Q7. Fill in the blank: A business idea must solve a ________.
Answer: Problem/need.
Q8. Why should workers avoid vague business ideas?
Answer: Because vague ideas create confusion and make planning difficult.
Q9. Example: A boda rider says, “I will carry passengers and deliver parcels.” Is this a business idea?
Answer: Yes — it clearly states the service to be offered.
Q10. Write down one business idea in your own words.
Answer: (Learner writes their own idea, e.g., “I will make and sell lunch packs to office workers.”)
Section 2: Customers (10 Q&A)
Q1. Who are customers in a business plan?
Answer: The people who will buy your product or service.
Q2. Why must a worker identify customers clearly?
Answer: Because different people have different needs and budgets.
Q3. Which of these is a clear customer group?
A) “Everybody.”
B) “Parents of school children in my town.”
C) “The whole world.”
Answer: B
Q4. True or False: Not every person is your customer.
Answer: True.
Q5. Why is it dangerous to think “everyone is my customer”?
Answer: Because you waste time and money trying to serve people who don’t need your product.
Q6. Example: A tailor’s main customers are _______.
Answer: Parents of school children.
Q7. Example: A boda rider’s main customers are _______.
Answer: Passengers, shops, and offices needing delivery.
Q8. How does knowing your customers help marketing?
Answer: You can advertise in the right places where your customers spend time.
Q9. Fill in the blank: A customer is someone who _______.
Answer: Pays for your product or service.
Q10. Write down one customer group for your own business idea.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “Office workers near the bus terminal.”)
Section 3: Competition (10 Q&A)
Q1. Who are competitors?
Answer: Other businesses selling similar products or services.
Q2. Why must you study competition?
Answer: To learn what they do well and where you can be different.
Q3. Example: A food seller’s competitor is _______.
Answer: Another food vendor selling in the same area.
Q4. Which of these is NOT competition?
A) Another tailor making uniforms.
B) A boda rider delivering parcels.
C) A customer buying food.
Answer: C
Q5. True or False: Competition is always bad.
Answer: False — competition can teach you useful lessons.
Q6. What makes you stand out from competitors?
Answer: Unique differences like price, quality, speed, or service.
Q7. Fill in the blank: If two tailors charge the same price, the one who _______ will attract more customers.
Answer: Delivers faster, or adds extra value.
Q8. Why is copying competitors dangerous?
Answer: Customers won’t see a reason to choose you if you look the same.
Q9. Which is a good example of standing out from competition?
A) Selling at the same price with no difference.
B) Adding free soup to rice meals while others don’t.
Answer: B
Q10. Write down one way you can be different from your competitors.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “I will deliver uniforms within 3 days.”)
Section 4: Costs & Pricing (10 Q&A)
Q1. What are costs in a business?
Answer: The money spent to make or deliver a product/service.
Q2. What is pricing?
Answer: The amount of money you charge customers for your product/service.
Q3. True or False: Pricing should always be lower than costs.
Answer: False — it must be higher to make a profit.
Q4. Which of these is a cost for a tailor?
A) Cloth
B) Thread
C) Transport
D) All of the above
Answer: D) All of the above
Q5. If a food vendor spends TSh 3,000 per plate and sells at TSh 4,000, what is the profit?
Answer: TSh 1,000 per plate.
Q6. Why is it important to calculate costs?
Answer: To set prices correctly and avoid losses.
Q7. What happens if you set your prices too low?
Answer: You may attract customers but lose money or make no profit.
Q8. What happens if you set prices too high?
Answer: Customers may choose cheaper competitors.
Q9. Fill in the blank: Costs tell you how much money goes ______, pricing tells you how much money comes ______.
Answer: Out; In.
Q10. Write down one cost in your own business.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “Charcoal for cooking.”)
Section 5: Marketing Plan (10 Q&A)
Q1. What is marketing?
Answer: Activities done to attract and keep customers.
Q2. Why is marketing important?
Answer: Customers cannot buy if they don’t know about your business.
Q3. Which of these is NOT a marketing method?
A) Flyers
B) Posters
C) Sleeping at home
D) Word of mouth
Answer: C) Sleeping at home
Q4. True or False: Marketing is only for big companies.
Answer: False — even small workers must market.
Q5. What is the cheapest and most powerful marketing tool?
Answer: Word of mouth (recommendations).
Q6. Example: A tailor markets by ____________.
Answer: Hanging uniforms outside her shop or sharing photos on WhatsApp.
Q7. Why is customer service part of marketing?
Answer: Happy customers return and bring others.
Q8. Fill in the blank: Marketing is about being _______ so customers remember you.
Answer: Visible.
Q9. Which is a smart marketing idea?
A) Posting work on social media.
B) Doing nothing and waiting for customers.
Answer: A
Q10. Write one way you can market your own product/service.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “Make flyers and share in offices.”)
Section 6: Operations Plan (10 Q&A)
Q1. What is an operations plan?
Answer: A guide showing how the business runs daily.
Q2. Why is it important?
Answer: It keeps work organized and efficient.
Q3. Which of these belongs in an operations plan?
A) Daily schedule
B) Suppliers
C) Tools needed
D) All of the above
Answer: D) All of the above
Q4. Example: A boda rider’s operations plan includes ____________.
Answer: Daily fueling, servicing bike weekly, and handling deliveries.
Q5. True or False: Operations only matter for big businesses.
Answer: False — every worker has daily operations.
Q6. What can go wrong if you don’t plan operations?
Answer: Delays, wasted time, unhappy customers.
Q7. Which worker shows good operations planning?
A) Food vendor who cooks at 11am.
B) Food vendor who cooks at 5am and is ready by 11am.
Answer: B
Q8. Fill in the blank: Operations planning makes sure you are _______ and _______.
Answer: Ready; Reliable.
Q9. What is one example of an operations detail for a tailor?
Answer: Cutting fabric in the morning, sewing in the afternoon.
Q10. Write one tool or supplier you need for your operations.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “Fuel station for my boda.”)
Section 7: Profit Goals (10 Q&A)
Q1. What is a profit goal?
Answer: The amount of money you want your business to earn after costs.
Q2. Why are profit goals important?
Answer: They motivate you and help track progress.
Q3. If costs = TSh 3,000 and selling price = TSh 4,000, profit is ________.
Answer: TSh 1,000.
Q4. True or False: Profit goals should be realistic and achievable.
Answer: True.
Q5. Which of these is a good monthly profit goal for a starter food vendor?
A) “I want TSh 20 million immediately.”
B) “I want TSh 400,000 after 1 month.”
Answer: B
Q6. Why must profit goals be written?
Answer: To measure success and stay focused.
Q7. Which is better?
A) “I hope to make something.”
B) “I want to earn TSh 500,000 this month.”
Answer: B
Q8. Fill in the blank: Profit = ______ – ______.
Answer: Selling price – Cost.
Q9. If a tailor earns TSh 4,000 profit per uniform and sells 30, monthly profit is ______.
Answer: TSh 120,000.
Q10. Write down one profit goal for your own business.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “Earn TSh 500,000 profit in 6 months.”)
Section 8: Funding Plan (10 Q&A)
Q1. What is funding?
Answer: The money used to start or grow a business.
Q2. Why is a funding plan important?
Answer: It shows where startup money will come from.
Q3. Which of these is NOT a safe funding source?
A) Savings
B) VICOBA loan
C) Loan shark with extreme interest
Answer: C
Q4. True or False: It is best to rely only on debt for funding.
Answer: False — combine savings and loans wisely.
Q5. Which of these shows good funding planning?
A) Tailor: “I will buy a machine from savings and cloth from deposits.”
B) Tailor: “I will just borrow without thinking.”
Answer: A
Q6. Why should you avoid unsafe loans?
Answer: High interest may trap you in debt.
Q7. Fill in the blank: Funding should come from safe sources like ______ and ______.
Answer: Savings; trusted loans (VICOBA, family, banks).
Q8. Example: A boda rider gets TSh 600,000 from savings and TSh 600,000 from VICOBA. What is this?
Answer: A funding plan.
Q9. What mistake do many workers make with funding?
Answer: Starting without knowing where all money will come from.
Q10. Write down one safe funding source for your business.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “My savings and a VICOBA loan.”)
Section 9: Team/Partnerships (10 Q&A)
Q1. What is a business team?
Answer: The people who help run or support your business.
Q2. Why are partnerships important?
Answer: They share workload, skills, and resources.
Q3. Example: Who is a partner for a food vendor?
Answer: Supplier of rice and beans.
Q4. True or False: Every business must be run alone.
Answer: False — teams make businesses stronger.
Q5. Which of these is a good partnership?
A) Having two suppliers in case one fails.
B) Trusting only one unreliable supplier.
Answer: A
Q6. Why must you choose reliable partners?
Answer: To avoid delays and losses.
Q7. Fill in the blank: A partnership is strong when both sides ______.
Answer: Benefit.
Q8. Which is an example of a personal team member?
Answer: A helper who irons uniforms.
Q9. What happens if a worker tries to do everything alone?
Answer: They may burn out and lose customers.
Q10. Write down one possible partner for your business.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “A boda rider to deliver uniforms.”)
Section 10: Risks & Solutions (10 Q&A)
Q1. What is a risk?
Answer: A problem that could go wrong and affect the business.
Q2. Why must workers think about risks?
Answer: To prepare solutions before problems happen.
Q3. True or False: All risks can be completely avoided.
Answer: False — but they can be reduced.
Q4. Example: If cloth prices rise, what is one solution?
Answer: Buy in bulk, use multiple suppliers.
Q5. Which of these is a financial risk?
A) Bike breakdown
B) Price of materials rising
C) A new competitor
Answer: B
Q6. Which of these is a customer risk?
A) Late payments
B) Tool breakdown
C) Illness of owner
Answer: A
Q7. Fill in the blank: Smart workers turn risks into ______.
Answer: Challenges with solutions.
Q8. Why should you have an emergency fund?
Answer: To cover unexpected costs.
Q9. Which is a good way to prepare for sickness risk?
Answer: Train a helper to cover work.
Q10. Write down one risk your business could face and one solution.
Answer: (Learner writes their own.)
Section 11: Growth Plan (10 Q&A)
Q1. What is a growth plan?
Answer: A step-by-step guide for expanding your business.
Q2. Why is growth important?
Answer: Businesses that don’t grow eventually struggle.
Q3. Which of these is a short-term growth step?
A) Adding juice to a food vendor’s menu.
B) Buying a second shop in another town.
Answer: A
Q4. Which of these is a long-term growth step?
A) Hiring an extra helper.
B) Opening a tailoring shop after 2 years.
Answer: B
Q5. True or False: Growth should be planned step by step.
Answer: True.
Q6. Fill in the blank: A growth plan should include ______, ______, and ______ goals.
Answer: Short-term, medium-term, long-term.
Q7. Example: A boda rider saves for a second motorbike in 2 years. What is this?
Answer: A growth plan.
Q8. What happens if a worker has no growth plan?
Answer: They remain stuck at the same level.
Q9. Why is reinvesting profit important?
Answer: It fuels growth instead of just survival.
Q10. Write down one growth step for your business in the next year.
Answer: (Learner writes, e.g., “Buy a second sewing machine.”)
📘 Lesson 4: Writing a Simple Plan

lesson_qa.question: Q1. What is the main purpose of writing a simple business plan?
options: A) To compete with big companies
B) To show off to friends
C) To organize your ideas clearly
D) To increase the number of pages

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) To organize your ideas clearly
Q2. What is the first step in writing a simple plan?
options: B) Write down your business idea

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Write down your business idea
Q3. Which of these is the best example of a simple business idea?
options: A) “I want to do something to earn.”
B) “I will make and sell uniforms to parents in my area.”
C) “I will try many different things.”
D) “I will copy my neighbor’s shop.”

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) “I will make and sell uniforms to parents in my area.”
Q4. Why should a simple plan be written down instead of kept in your head?
options: B) You may forget important details if not written

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) You may forget important details if not written
Q5. Which of these parts must be included in a simple plan?
options: B) Business idea, customers, costs, profit goals

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Business idea, customers, costs, profit goals
Q6. True or False: A one-page plan can still be effective.
Answer: A) True
Q7. If you don’t write a plan, what may happen?
options: B) You may waste money and get confused
C) Your costs will go down automatically

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) You may waste money and get confused
Q8. Which of these is an example of setting a profit goal in your plan?
options: A) “I will sell food to everyone.”
B) “I want to earn TSh 400,000 profit per month.”
C) “I will wait and see what happens.”
D) “I will work like my neighbor.”

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) “I want to earn TSh 400,000 profit per month.”
Q9. What should you write under the ‘customers’ section of your plan?
options: C) “Specific groups like parents, office workers, or shops”

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) “Specific groups like parents, office workers, or shops”
Q10. Why should your plan include competitors?
options: B) To understand their strengths and weaknesses
C) To copy everything they do

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To understand their strengths and weaknesses
Q11. Which is the correct order when writing a simple plan?
options: A) Write business idea → Identify customers → Calculate costs/profits → Set goals
B) Borrow money → Start business → Then write plan
C) Write hobbies → Guess prices → Buy stock → Wait
D) Wait until you have many customers, then plan

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Write business idea → Identify customers → Calculate costs/profits → Set goals
Q12. What is one danger of making your plan too complicated?
options: B) You may confuse yourself and give up

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) You may confuse yourself and give up
Q13. Which of these is a good short-term goal to include in a simple plan?
options: A) “I want to open 20 shops in one year.”
B) “I want to sell 25 plates of food daily within 3 months.”
C) “I will become the richest in my town tomorrow.”
D) “I want to copy my competitor’s price list.”

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) “I want to sell 25 plates of food daily within 3 months.”
Q14. Why should you review your simple plan regularly?
options: A) To increase the number of pages
B) To see if you are still on track and adjust if needed

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To see if you are still on track and adjust if needed
Q15. Fill in the blank: A simple business plan is like a ______ because it guides you step by step.
Answer: A) Recipe
📘 Lesson 5: Setting Realistic Goals
Q1. What does it mean to set a realistic goal?
options: A) A goal that is impossible to achieve
B) A goal that can be reached with effort and planning
C) A goal that requires no work
D) A goal copied from competitors

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) A goal that can be reached with effort and planning
Q2. Which of these is a good example of a realistic short-term goal?
options: A) “I want to sell 20 uniforms this month.”
B) “I want to own 50 shops tomorrow.”
C) “I want to be the richest in the world.”
D) “I want to fly to space next week.”

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) “I want to sell 20 uniforms this month.”
Q3. Why should goals be written down?
options: A) To make the plan look long
B) To remind you and measure progress

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To remind you and measure progress
Q4. True or False: Setting goals too high may cause disappointment and quitting.
Answer: A) True
Q5. Which of these is a SMART goal?
options: B) “I will sell 30 lunch packs daily within 2 months.”
C) “I want to be successful one day.”
D) “I will copy my competitor’s sales.”

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) “I will sell 30 lunch packs daily within 2 months.”
Q6. What does the “R” in SMART goals stand for?
Answer: B) Realistic
Q7. Which of these is NOT a reason to set goals?
options: A) To guide your daily work

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) To waste your time
Q8. Example: A boda rider sets a goal to save TSh 200,000 in 6 months for bike repairs. This is ________.
options: A) A clear and realistic goal

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) A clear and realistic goal
Q9. What type of goals should be included in a plan?
options: A) Goals that are too easy
C) Goals that balance challenge and possibility

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) Goals that balance challenge and possibility
Q10. Which worker has set a realistic goal?
options: A) Food seller: “I want to sell 500 plates in one day.”
B) Tailor: “I will make 10 uniforms this week.”
C) Mason: “I will build 20 houses this month.”
D) Rider: “I will earn 10 million tomorrow.”

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Tailor: “I will make 10 uniforms this week.”
Q11. Fill in the blank: Goals should be ______ and ______.
Answer: B) Clear; realistic
Q12. Why are small goals important?
options: A) They are easy to ignore
B) They help you move step by step toward bigger goals
C) They stop you from working hard
D) They are only for new workers

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) They help you move step by step toward bigger goals
Q13. Which of these shows a medium-term goal?
options: A) Buy 2nd sewing machine after 12 months
C) Sell 1 plate of food today

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Buy 2nd sewing machine after 12 months
Q14. What should you do if you don’t reach your goal the first time?
options: C) Adjust the goal and try again

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) Adjust the goal and try again
Q15. Why do customers trust workers with clear goals?
options: A) Because they know the worker is serious and reliable
B) Because the worker promises anything
C) Because the worker copies competitors
D) Because the worker hides information

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Because they know the worker is serious and reliable
📘 Lesson 6: Estimating Costs & Profits
Q1. What are business costs?
options: A) The money you spend to run your business
B) The money you keep as profit
D) The money you borrow from VICOBA

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) The money you spend to run your business
Q2. What is profit?
options: A) The total money customers pay
B) The difference between sales and costs
C) The money used for transport
D) Free items given to customers

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) The difference between sales and costs
Q3. If a food vendor spends TSh 3,000 on one plate and sells it for TSh 4,000, what is the profit?
Answer: A) TSh 1,000
Q4. Which of these is a fixed cost?
options: B) Amount of rice used daily

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Rent for your shop
Q5. Which of these is a variable cost?
options: B) Daily fuel for a boda rider

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Daily fuel for a boda rider
Q6. True or False: Profits are guaranteed even if you don’t calculate costs properly.
Answer: B) False
Q7. Why should workers estimate costs before starting a business?
options: B) To set the right price and avoid losses
D) To make the plan longer

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To set the right price and avoid losses
Q8. Which of these formulas is correct?
Answer: B) Profit = Price – Costs
Q9. If a tailor spends TSh 11,000 to make a uniform and sells it for TSh 15,000, profit is ______.
Answer: B) TSh 4,000
Q10. What happens if your prices are lower than your costs?
options: D) You attract more free customers

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) You lose money
Q11. Which of these is an example of underestimating costs?
options: A) Forgetting to include fuel in delivery costs
B) Writing down all expenses clearly
C) Including packaging in daily costs
D) Adding supplier costs into the plan

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Forgetting to include fuel in delivery costs
Q12. Why is it useful to separate fixed and variable costs?
options: A) To make the plan look longer
B) To see which costs change with sales and which stay constant

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To see which costs change with sales and which stay constant
Q13. If you sell 30 uniforms at TSh 15,000 each and cost per uniform is TSh 11,000, what is the total profit?
Answer: A) TSh 120,000
Q14. Why should you review your profits regularly?
options: A) To hide them from competitors
B) To see if your goals are being met and adjust if needed

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To see if your goals are being met and adjust if needed
Q15. Fill in the blank: Profits grow when you ______ costs and ______ sales.
Answer: B) Reduce; increase
📘 Lesson 7: Marketing & Customer Attraction
Q1. What is marketing?
options: B) Activities done to attract and keep customers
C) Borrowing money for the business
D) Hiring competitors to help you

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Activities done to attract and keep customers
Q2. Why is marketing important?
options: A) Customers cannot buy if they don’t know about your business

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Customers cannot buy if they don’t know about your business
Q3. Which of these is the cheapest and most powerful marketing tool?
Answer: B) Word of mouth
Q4. True or False: Marketing is only for big companies.
Answer: B) False
Q5. Which of these is an example of simple marketing for a food vendor?
options: A) Hanging a clear sign near the bus stand

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Hanging a clear sign near the bus stand
Q6. What does customer service have to do with marketing?
options: B) Happy customers return and recommend you to others

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Happy customers return and recommend you to others
Q7. Which of these is NOT a marketing method?
options: C) Sitting at home without working

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) Sitting at home without working
Q8. Why is knowing your customers important for marketing?
options: A) It helps you advertise in the right places

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) It helps you advertise in the right places
Q9. Example: A tailor shares uniform photos in WhatsApp groups. This is ________.
Answer: B) Advertising
Q10. Which of these is an advantage of using Jinnar for marketing?
options: A) Customers can see your profile and ratings

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Customers can see your profile and ratings
Q11. Fill in the blank: Marketing is about being ______ so customers remember you.
Answer: B) Visible
Q12. Which is a smart marketing idea for a boda rider?
options: A) Give a business card with phone number to customers

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Give a business card with phone number to customers
Q13. Why is offering something extra (like free soup or ironing) part of marketing?
options: B) It makes you look generous and different from competitors
C) It reduces your profit always

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) It makes you look generous and different from competitors
Q14. Which customer is more likely to stay loyal?
options: B) One who is treated with respect and good service
D) One who is delayed daily

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) One who is treated with respect and good service
Q15. What happens if you do no marketing at all?
options: A) Customers will still find you easily
B) Competitors will send you their customers
C) Your business may remain unknown and fail
D) Your costs will reduce automatically

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) Your business may remain unknown and fail
📘 Lesson 8: Risk and Problem Planning
Q1. What is a business risk?
options: B) A problem that might affect your business
C) A gift from a customer

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) A problem that might affect your business
Q2. Why must workers think about risks?
options: B) To prepare solutions before problems happen
C) To make their plan longer

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To prepare solutions before problems happen
Q3. True or False: All risks can be completely avoided.
Answer: B) False
Q4. Which of these is a financial risk?
Answer: D) All of the above
Q5. Which of these is an operational risk?
options: A) Supplier fails to deliver on time

lesson_qa.question: Answer: D) All of the above
Q6. Which is the best way to reduce customer late-payment risk?
options: A) Give them all goods on credit
B) Refuse to sell to anyone
C) Ask for deposits before starting work

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) Ask for deposits before starting work
Q7. Example: A boda rider keeps two mechanics instead of one. This is an example of ________.
Answer: B) Risk planning
Q8. Which of these is a health-related risk for workers?
options: A) Getting sick and being unable to work

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Getting sick and being unable to work
Q9. Why should workers save part of their profits?
options: B) To pay for emergencies and risks
D) To hide money from family

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To pay for emergencies and risks
Q10. Fill in the blank: Risks cannot be removed, but they can be ________.
Answer: B) Reduced
Q11. Which of these shows poor risk planning?
options: B) Having a backup boda rider

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) Ignoring all risks
Q12. Why should risks be written in a plan?
options: B) To remind you of problems and solutions

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) To remind you of problems and solutions
Q13. If a tailor depends on only one supplier, what risk does she face?
Answer: B) Supplier risk
Q14. Which of these is a good solution to rising costs of materials?
options: B) Buy in bulk or switch suppliers

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Buy in bulk or switch suppliers
Q15. Why do smart workers see risks as challenges?
options: A) Because challenges help them find solutions and grow stronger

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Because challenges help them find solutions and grow stronger
📘 Lesson 9: Teamwork and Partnerships
Q1. What does teamwork in business mean?
options: A) Workers competing with each other
B) People working together toward the same goal

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) People working together toward the same goal
Q2. Why are partnerships important in small businesses?
options: B) They allow sharing of workload, skills, and resources

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) They allow sharing of workload, skills, and resources
Q3. Which of these is an example of a partnership?
options: A) A food vendor teaming up with a boda rider for deliveries
D) A mason hiding his workers

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) A food vendor teaming up with a boda rider for deliveries
Q4. True or False: Every successful business is built only by one person with no help.
Answer: B) False
Q5. Which of these shows good teamwork?
options: A) A helper measuring cloth while the tailor sews
B) A rider refusing to deliver for shops
C) A food seller hiding her cooking secrets from helpers
D) A mason refusing to delegate work

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) A helper measuring cloth while the tailor sews
Q6. Why should workers choose reliable partners?
options: A) To avoid delays and problems
D) To make customers pay more

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) To avoid delays and problems
Q7. Which of these is a customer-facing partnership?
options: A) A tailor and fabric supplier
B) A boda rider and mechanic
C) A food vendor and office group ordering lunch packs
D) A mason and his apprentice

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) A food vendor and office group ordering lunch packs
Q8. Fill in the blank: A strong partnership is when both sides ______.
Answer: B) Benefit
Q9. Which of these is NOT teamwork?
options: C) Competing against your own partner

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) Competing against your own partner
Q10. What is one risk of poor teamwork?
Answer: B) Delays and unhappy customers
Q11. Which partnership is more reliable?
options: A) Depending only on one unreliable supplier
B) Having two suppliers for cloth

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Having two suppliers for cloth
Q12. Why does teamwork build customer trust?
options: A) Customers notice smooth cooperation and feel respected
B) Customers don’t care about teamwork
C) Customers always prefer solo workers

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Customers notice smooth cooperation and feel respected
Q13. Which of these is a long-term partnership example?
options: A) Tailor and parent buying one uniform
B) Rider and office signing delivery contract
C) Food vendor selling one plate once
D) Mason working one day only

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Rider and office signing delivery contract
Q14. True or False: Partnerships can help workers grow faster.
Answer: A) True
Q15. Why is teamwork especially important for growth?
options: A) Because no one can succeed alone for long
B) Because it reduces competition completely
C) Because it makes profits appear instantly
D) Because it avoids all risks

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Because no one can succeed alone for long
📘 Lesson 10: Growth and Expansion
Q1. What does business growth mean?
options: B) Step-by-step increase in sales, profit, or size

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Step-by-step increase in sales, profit, or size
Q2. Why is growth important for workers?
options: A) To spend more money carelessly
B) To show off to neighbors
C) To stay competitive and improve income

lesson_qa.question: Answer: C) To stay competitive and improve income
Q3. Which of these is a short-term growth step?
options: A) A tailor buying extra fabric stock for busy season
B) A food vendor opening 10 branches overnight
C) A boda rider buying 5 new bikes tomorrow
D) A mason building 50 houses in one week

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) A tailor buying extra fabric stock for busy season
Q4. Which of these is a medium-term growth plan?
options: A) Expanding from 25 to 50 customers in 6 months
B) Becoming the richest in the country tomorrow

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Expanding from 25 to 50 customers in 6 months
Q5. Which of these is a long-term growth plan?
options: A) Food vendor expanding into catering within 2 years
B) Tailor making one uniform today

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Food vendor expanding into catering within 2 years
Q6. True or False: Growth should be planned step by step (short, medium, long-term).
Answer: A) True
Q7. Fill in the blank: Growth requires reinvesting ______.
Answer: C) Profits
Q8. Which of these shows smart reinvestment?
options: A) A tailor uses profits to buy a second sewing machine
B) A rider spends all profits on luxury items
C) A food vendor hides money without investing
D) A mason stops saving for tools

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) A tailor uses profits to buy a second sewing machine
Q9. What happens if a worker never plans for growth?
options: A) The business may remain small or fail

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) The business may remain small or fail
Q10. Which of these growth actions is realistic?
options: A) Rider saving for a second motorbike in 2 years
B) Rider buying 20 motorbikes tomorrow without money
C) Tailor opening 50 shops next week
D) Food vendor selling 1,000 plates in one day

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Rider saving for a second motorbike in 2 years
Q11. Why should growth plans be realistic?
options: A) Unrealistic goals may cause disappointment and quitting
C) Competitors disappear when you dream big
D) It makes your plan longer

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Unrealistic goals may cause disappointment and quitting
Q12. Which is an example of expanding services?
options: A) Food vendor adding juice to the menu
B) Tailor refusing to sew new designs

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Food vendor adding juice to the menu
Q13. Which is an example of expanding customers?
options: A) Tailor selling to one family only
B) Rider offering services to offices as well as passengers
C) Food vendor selling only to neighbors

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Rider offering services to offices as well as passengers
Q14. Which is an example of expanding location?
options: A) Tailor opening a shop near a school after 2 years
B) Rider refusing to leave his village
C) Food vendor serving from the same spot forever
D) Mason working only at home

lesson_qa.question: Answer: A) Tailor opening a shop near a school after 2 years
Q15. Why is growth part of every business plan?
options: A) Because workers must show off
B) Because without growth, businesses may remain weak
C) Because banks demand many pages

lesson_qa.question: Answer: B) Because without growth, businesses may remain weak
